title: Instructions, Context, Input, Output
concept: The four building blocks of a well-formed prompt and how each shapes the response.

exercises.prompt: Before we dive in: when you ask a friend for directions, what usually makes their directions actually useful to you?

options.text: They give lots of details like landmarks and turns, tailored to where you're starting from
They just say 'go that way' and walk off
They repeat the destination name five times

exercises.explain: Useful directions include specific, relevant details tailored to your situation — just like a useful AI prompt needs specific details tailored to your task. Vague instructions, whether from a friend or to an AI, lead to guessing.

cards.title: Why Do Some Prompts Just... Work?
cards.body: Ever notice how some people get amazing AI answers on the first try, while others get vague junk? It's rarely luck. Great prompts are built from the same four ingredients, every time.

cards.title: The Four Building Blocks
cards.body: A well-formed prompt usually has: INSTRUCTIONS (the task to do), CONTEXT (background info that shapes the answer), INPUT (the actual content to work on), and OUTPUT (the format or style you want back). Together they tell the AI exactly what to do and how to do it.

cards.title: A Prompt Is Like a Sandwich Order
cards.body: At a deli counter you say: what you want (instructions), why you want it a certain way like 'I'm allergic to nuts' (context), the specific order details like 'on rye bread' (input), and how you want it wrapped or served (output). Skip a part and the sandwich maker has to guess.

cards.title: Turning a Vague Ask Into a Full Prompt
cards.body: Vague: 'Write an email.' Full prompt: Instructions: 'Write a follow-up email.' Context: 'To a client who missed our meeting yesterday.' Input: 'Meeting was about the Q3 budget.' Output: 'Keep it under 100 words, friendly tone.' The second version gives the AI a clear target instead of a guess.

cards.title: Myth: Longer Prompts Are Always Better
cards.body: Not true! A prompt can be long but still missing a key block, like output format, and still confuse the AI. What matters is covering all four building blocks clearly, not piling on extra words. A tight, complete prompt beats a rambling incomplete one.

cards.title: Bare Prompt vs. Full Prompt
cards.body: Compare a one-line request with a fully built prompt using all four blocks.

left.text: 'Summarize this.' — The AI has to guess the length, tone, and audience.

right.text: 'Summarize this article in 2 sentences for a 10-year-old, using simple words.' — Every block is covered, so the result matches your intent.

exercises.prompt: Which part of a prompt gives the AI background info that shapes how it should respond, like 'the reader is a 5-year-old'?
exercises.explain: Context is background information that shapes the response without being the task itself or the raw content. Saying 'the reader is a 5-year-old' tells the AI HOW to adjust its explanation, which is exactly what context does.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Every good prompt must always include all four blocks (instructions, context, input, output) explicitly written out, even for the simplest questions.
exercises.explain: False. Simple questions like 'What's the capital of France?' don't need context or output formatting because the instruction alone is clear. The four blocks are a toolkit to reach for when a task is ambiguous or complex, not a rigid checklist for every single prompt.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing building block.
exercises.sentence: Telling the AI 'respond in exactly three bullet points' is an example of specifying the output.
exercises.explain: Describing the shape, length, or format of the answer (like 'three bullet points') controls the OUTPUT. It doesn't change the task itself, just how the final answer should look.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three prompts was most likely AI-generated as a demonstration of a 'complete' prompt (rather than written quickly by a rushed human)?

options.text: Summarize this article in 3 bullet points for a busy manager who only has 30 seconds; article text: [pasted text]; tone: neutral and factual.

exercises.explain: Option B is unusually structured and thorough — it explicitly labels the audience (context), the task (instructions), the content (input), and the format (output) almost like a template. Real quick human prompts tend to look like A and C: short, casual, and missing pieces, because people usually don't spell out every block unless they're deliberately demonstrating good prompt structure.

exercises.prompt: You want an AI to write a birthday message. Which prompt uses ALL FOUR building blocks (instructions, context, input, output) correctly?

options.text: Write a short birthday message for my friend Sam, who loves hiking and just turned 30. Keep it under 30 words and make it upbeat.
You are a helpful assistant that writes messages.

exercises.explain: Option B has everything: the INSTRUCTION (write a birthday message), the CONTEXT (friend Sam, loves hiking, turning 30), the INPUT (the specific facts about Sam), and the OUTPUT format (short, under 30 words, upbeat tone). The other options are missing key pieces — A has no context or input, C is just fragmented facts with no clear instruction or output shape, and D only sets a role with no task at all. When you include all four blocks, the AI has everything it needs to produce exactly what you pictured in your head.
